evals.name: add-pagination-to-new-endpoint
evals.prompt: Add a new GET /api/v1/addresses endpoint that returns all client addresses paginated. Default page size 25, max 200. Allow sorting by clientId and seq only. Reuse our existing patterns — look at the clients search endpoint for reference. Return the result the way Spring normally returns a Page. No separate address service needs to exist yet — you can add the minimum wiring required (service method + repo method) to make the controller work, using the same module boundaries the rest of the project uses.
evals.expected_output: A controller method on a new or existing controller that accepts Pageable with @PageableDefault(size = 25), returns ResponseEntity<Page<AddressDto>>, enforces max size = 200, and only honors sorts on clientId and seq (other sort fields should be rejected or silently dropped). Repository method returns Page<AddressEntity> and uses @EntityGraph if the DTO reads any lazy association on ClientEntity. Service passes Pageable through. No manual PageImpl rebuild.

assertions.name: controller-returns-page-of-dto
assertions.text: The controller method returns ResponseEntity<Page<...>> with a DTO type parameter — not List<>, not a custom response envelope.

assertions.name: controller-uses-pageable-default-size-25
assertions.text: The Pageable parameter on the controller method is annotated with @PageableDefault(size = 25).

assertions.name: max-page-size-capped-at-200
assertions.text: Max page size is enforced at 200 somewhere — either via spring.data.web.pageable.max-page-size in application.yml, or per-endpoint via a size-validation mechanism. Unbounded max fails.

assertions.name: sort-whitelist-clientid-and-seq-only
assertions.text: Only sorts on clientId and seq are honored. Any other sort field is dropped (not rejected with 400).

assertions.name: service-passes-pageable-through
assertions.text: The service method accepts Pageable and passes it through to the repository. No materializing a List and slicing.

assertions.name: uses-page-map-for-dto-mapping
assertions.text: Mapping from entity-page to DTO-page uses page.map(mapper::toDto) (or equivalent). No manual new PageImpl<>(...) rebuild.

assertions.name: respects-module-boundaries
assertions.text: New classes land in the correct modules per CLAUDE.md (controller + DTO + mapper in api, service + repo interface in core, repo impl in infrastructure).

evals.name: fix-n-plus-one-on-paged-search
evals.prompt: Something is wrong with GET /api/v1/clients?size=100. When I watch the Postgres logs I see 101 queries per request — 1 for the client page, then 100 more, one per client, each loading that client's addresses. The DTO includes the addresses list. This wasn't an issue when we only had a handful of clients but it's starting to hurt. Diagnose and fix. Point me to the exact file(s) that need to change and explain why your fix eliminates the extra queries.
evals.expected_output: Identifies this as classic N+1 caused by lazy loading of client.addresses on each row of the page. Fix is @EntityGraph(attributePaths = {"addresses"}) on ClientRepository.search (or the Specification-based search method), so Hibernate joins addresses in the same query. Bonus: mentions that open-in-view: false in this project means lazy access outside the service boundary would actually throw, making @EntityGraph the only correct fix.

assertions.name: diagnosis-names-n-plus-one-with-lazy-addresses
assertions.text: DIAGNOSIS.md names the problem as an N+1 caused by lazy-loading of client.addresses when the mapper iterates the page.

assertions.name: fix-uses-entity-graph-on-addresses
assertions.text: The fix adds @EntityGraph(attributePaths = {"addresses"}) (or equivalent) on the ClientRepository search method.

assertions.name: fix-targets-repository-not-dto-or-mapper
assertions.text: Modified file(s) are at the repository layer. The Client DTO and ClientMapper are not changed.

assertions.name: explanation-predicts-single-joined-query
assertions.text: EXPLANATION.md says Hibernate will emit a single query (LEFT JOIN on addresses) instead of 1+N after the fix.

assertions.name: mentions-open-in-view-false-implication
assertions.text: Calls out that open-in-view: false makes session-scoped lazy loading unsafe outside the service boundary, reinforcing @EntityGraph as the right fix.

assertions.name: no-behavior-change-to-dto-shape
assertions.text: The Client DTO is unchanged — addresses still appear in the response exactly as before.

evals.name: reject-unsafe-sort
evals.prompt: Security review pointed out something I hadn't thought about: GET /api/v1/clients?sort=email,asc works. So does ?sort=phoneNumber,desc. Basically any field on ClientEntity is sortable externally, including anything internal we might add later. They want us to restrict sortable fields to an explicit whitelist: firstName, lastName, and id only. Anything else should be silently dropped (not 400) so existing clients don't break. Implement this for the clients search endpoint. Also: they noticed pagination is sometimes unstable — the same row shows up on page 2 and page 3 when sorting by lastName. Fix that too.
evals.expected_output: Sort-sanitization logic applied to the search endpoint that filters Pageable.getSort() against a whitelist {firstName, lastName, id}, dropping (not rejecting) any other order. A stable-sort tiebreaker is appended — id — so rows never appear on two pages when the sort column has ties. Reasonably placed (controller adapter, a small @ModelAttribute resolver, or a utility method) — not scattered.

assertions.name: whitelist-is-firstname-lastname-id
assertions.text: Allowed sort fields are exactly firstName, lastName, id.

assertions.name: unknown-sorts-dropped-not-400
assertions.text: Unknown sort fields are silently dropped. No 400 returned.

assertions.name: stable-tiebreaker-on-id-appended
assertions.text: id is appended as a tiebreaker on the sanitized Sort.

assertions.name: whitelist-lives-at-adapter-boundary
assertions.text: Sanitization is at a boundary (controller / resolver / utility) — not in the entity, not duplicated across layers.

assertions.name: existing-firstname-sort-still-works
assertions.text: An existing ?sort=firstName,asc request still works as before.
